coredata.dc:title: Study on the influence of magnetic field on the performance of lithium-ion batteries

coredata.prism:issueName: 2021 International Conference on New Energy and Power Engineering
coredata.dc:creator: Ruan, Guanqiang
Hua, Jing
Hu, Xing
Yu, Changqing
coredata.dc:description: As the power source of new energy vehicles, the impact of battery performance should be considered. The magnetic field is generated by the change of the moving charge or the electric field. The magnetic field could magnetize the battery, and many small magnetic dipoles appear. Therefore, an experimental method of charge and discharge performance test and internal resistance test imposing magnetic field effect was conducted. Then the effect of the magnetic field effect on the charge–discharge performance and internal resistance of widely used 18650 lithium-ion battery was studied. The results showed that the trends of terminal voltage during charge and discharge process with and without magnetic field are basically the same. And the charge–discharge performance with imposing the magnetic field is much better. The discharge capacity, charge capacity, discharge energy and charge energy increase obviously while the magnetic induction intensity increases. This study can effectively help to solve the problem of short range of new energy vehicles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic field effect
Lithium-ion battery
Performance

Charge and discharge test method with magnetic field effect
Internal resistance test method without magnetic field effect
Internal resistance test method with magnetic field effect

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of experimental platform structure.
Gripping mode and installation of lithium battery.
The position of a single lithium-ion battery in a magnetic field.
(a) Discharge capacity curve of battery at different rates; (b) Charge capacity curve of battery at different rates.
(a) Discharge energy curve of battery at different rates; (b) Charge energy curve of battery at different rates.
Discharge curve under different magnetic induction intensity at 1/3C rate.
(a) Ohmic resistance varies with SOC; (b) Polarization resistance varies with SOC.
Main parameters of the 18650 lithium battery.

ce:dochead.ce:textfn: 2021 International Conference on New Energy and Power Engineering (ICNEPE 2021) November 19 to 21, 2021, Sanya, China

head.ce:title: Study on the influence of magnetic field on the performance of lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Shanghai Dianji University, Shanghai 201306, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical Engineering, Shanghai Dianji University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering,Shanghai Dianji University ,Shanghai 201306,China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As the power source of new energy vehicles, the impact of battery performance should be considered. The magnetic field is generated by the change of the moving charge or the electric field. The magnetic field could magnetize the battery, and many small magnetic dipoles appear. Therefore, an experimental method of charge and discharge performance test and internal resistance test imposing magnetic field effect was conducted. Then the effect of the magnetic field effect on the charge–discharge performance and internal resistance of widely used 18650 lithium-ion battery was studied. The results showed that the trends of terminal voltage during charge and discharge process with and without magnetic field are basically the same. And the charge–discharge performance with imposing the magnetic field is much better. The discharge capacity, charge capacity, discharge energy and charge energy increase obviously while the magnetic induction intensity increases. This study can effectively help to solve the problem of short range of new energy vehicles.

ce:section.ce:para: With the emergence of the shortage of fossil fuels, environmental pollution, and global warming, governments of various countries have actively responded to the call and put forward their own development strategies. As the power source of new energy vehicle, the lithium-ion battery’s main responsibility is to provide continuous operation power for new energy automobiles and ensure the safe, stable, economical and green operation of vehicles. Lithium-ion batteries are well accepted in electric vehicles with the good quality of small size, large capacity, light weight, no pollution, and long cycle life [1,2].

The impact of battery performance should be considered in the research on electric vehicles. However, the current researches on the charge and discharge performance under different charge and discharge modes were carried out under normal conditions. Authors in [3] studied the influence of different thermal conditions on the performance of lithium-ion batteries, and found that the discharge capacity of the battery decreased significantly with the increase of the discharge rate under the constant temperature of 20 °C, and the discharge capacity had nothing to do with the discharge rate near the adiabatic state. Authors in [4] proved that the Lorentz force, which caused electron and ion drift during charging and discharging of lithium batteries, could increase the capacity. Authors in [5] studied the influences of lithium-ion battery cycles on the SEI layer, the state of charge of the anode and its response to the external magnetic field. And they found that the response to magnetic field attenuated as the number of cycles increased. However, there were still relatively few researches about the influence of magnetic field on the performances of lithium-ion batteries.

Magnetic field effect could affect the lithium-ion batteries performance. The magnetic field magnetize the battery, and many small magnetic dipoles appear, so that the particles in the battery have magnetic arrangement, and then the ionic conductivity is improved, and the flow and diffusion of ions are accelerated. In addition, magnetic field applied to microorganisms can produce biological reactions, which could cause macro microbial magnetic effect [6,7], so as to affect the chemical reaction rate in the charge–discharge process. Authors in [8] applied an easy and simple method to impose a magnetic field to the anode of a lithium-ion battery. Li-ions suffered the Lorentz force to cause the spiral motion to produce an MHD effect. And this kind of MHD effect promoted the transfer of the mass, decreased the lithium-ion concentration gradient, and reduced concentration difference to restrain dendrite growth to get an evenly arranged lithium layer. The results showed that the lithium-ion battery electrodes under the magnetic field exhibited great cycling and rate behavior in a symmetrical battery. Authors in [9] applied a magnetic field to the anode of lithium-ion batteries. They found that the electrochemical properties of a battery can be greatly improved by making anodes with an out-of-plane alignment structure under a low applied external magnetic field. Authors in [10] studied and analyzed the change of water properties in proton exchange membrane fuel cell under magnetic effect through Bragg grating. The results illustrated that the evaporation rate of the magnetized water in the fuel cell was increased, and the temperature and humidity in the cell were improved, thus the performance of the cell was improved.

Therefore, this paper designed an experimental method of charge and discharge performance test and internal resistance test imposing magnetic field effect. Then the effect of the magnetic field effect on the charge–discharge performance and internal resistance of widely used 18650 lithium-ion battery was studied, and an effective solution for the short range of electric vehicles was proposed.

In order to study the charge–discharge performance and internal resistance properties of lithium-ion batteries imposing magnetic field effect, an experimental system was built. The experimental platform is composed of lithium-ion batteries, a charge–discharge test system, and a DC magnetic field generation system. The structure of the experimental platform is shown in Fig. 1. Lithium-ion batteries provide experimental objects for the experiments. The charge and discharge system can monitor the changes in voltage, current, capacity and other data of lithium batteries during the experiment. It is convenient for us to study the charge–discharge performance and internal resistance properties. The DC magnetic field generation system provides a uniform DC magnetic field for the experiments, which is beneficial to figure out the influence of magnetic field effect on lithium-ion battery.

During all the experiments, the equipment was operated in a thermostat, and the temperature was maintained at 25 °C. Firstly, the 18650 lithium-ion battery was connected to the battery test system by using the battery clamp of MACCOR device, and the battery clamp and battery were put into the Helmholtz coil device. Then, the charge–discharge experiment and internal resistance experiment of the 18650 battery with specific rate were carried out through the computer program in advance, and the changes of voltage, current and related states in the process of experiment were monitored and recorded in real time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Basic parameters and fixture system of lithium battery
ce:section.ce:para:
The cathode material of the usually involved 18650 lithium-ion batteries generally include lithium cobalt oxide, nickel cobalt manganese ternary, nickel cobalt aluminum ternary and so on. Under the condition of high voltage charge and discharge, due to the phase transition and lattice oxygen loss of materials in high delithiation state, resulting in structural instability, poor cycling behavior and thermal stability of the lithium cobalt oxide battery, which limits the application of LiCoO2 in large-scale lithium-ion battery [11]. Ternary materials have good rate performance. Authors in [12] conducted a comparative research of the electrochemical behavior of LiCo
1/3
Ni
1/3
Mn
1/3
O2 and LiCoO2. The results illustrated that LiCo
1/3
Ni
1/3
Mn
1/3
O2 has good high rate discharge performance and cycle performance. At present, the ternary lithium-ion battery is favored by major automobile companies because of its good cycle performance and good thermal stability. For the purpose of studying the performance of the battery to be tested in the magnetic field, the battery used is the 18650 cylindrical lithium-ion battery. The cathode material is nickel cobalt aluminum ternary material, and the anode material is artificial graphite. The main parameters of the used 18650 lithium-ion battery are listed in Table 1.

A suitable and feasible battery fixture is a very important key factor to ensure the lithium-ion battery performance test. Considering that some lithium-ion batteries will accumulate too many side reaction products during the charge and discharge process, especially
H
2
, CO, CO2 and other gases [13,14]. This will cause the expansion and bulging of batteries [15,16]. So it is necessary to install a specific fixture before testing. In addition, because the cylindrical lithium-ion battery is difficult to join with the charge and discharge equipment directly, it is also necessary to fix it with a special clamp. Fig. 2 shows the clamping method. The B + and B − marked on the fixture indicate the positive and negative of the current respectively, and S + and S − indicate the positive and negative of the voltage respectively. During the experiments, only the positive electrode of the battery needs to be connected to the B + and S + of the fixture, and the negative electrode is attached to B − and S − . Then the voltage and current can be measured in real time through MACCOR’s exclusive channel.

The test system is mainly composed of American MACCOR battery test equipment, the 18650 lithium-ion battery and upper computer. The MACCOR charge–discharge performance test device can directly and effectively obtain the current, voltage, capacity, energy and other data of lithium-ion battery by loading specific test procedures or vehicle working conditions, so as to investigate whether the tested battery could meet the demands of electric vehicles. During the experiment, one of the battery test channels is selected to carry out the charge–discharge experiment. The battery is inserted into the channel and the connection between the battery and the test line of the channel is confirmed. Under the control of computer program, the charge and discharge experiment can be carried out under various conditions.

MACCOR equipment includes two parts: hardware and software. The hardware is mainly composed of a host, a display, and a testing machine. The software is mainly a special MACCOR software, which issues programming instructions to the MACCOR battery test equipment through the network port and receives experimental data in real time. The desktop of the tester provides 8 battery test channels, it is supported by the MACCOR standard test system software to conduct charge–discharge experiments. The test voltage range is ± 5 V or 0 V 5 V, and the voltage accuracy can reach ± 0.02%. The test current has four ranges of 5 A, 150 mA, 5 mA, and 150 μ A. The data acquisition time can reach 1 ms.

The magnetic field generating device is mainly composed of Helmholtz coil equipment. The Helmholtz coil is a pair of parallel and connected coaxial circular coils. The coils are connected in series to form a closed coil without head and tail. The current directions of the two coils are the same and the sizes are the same. When the direct current passes through the coil, a magnetic field will be generated. Because the distance between the two coils is equal to the radius of each coil, the total magnetic field produced by the two coils is uniform in a large range near the midpoint of the axis, which can produce a DC magnetic field with specified volume ratio, high uniformity and weak magnetic field value [17,18].

The magnetic field generator adopts the PS1LXG-280 Helmholtz coil produced in Hunan province of China. The number of coil turns is 1488 and the coil radius is 60 mm. As the Helmholtz coil has the open nature, the battery can be easily inserted or removed, and visual observation can also be done directly to facilitate the study of the performance characteristics of the battery imposing magnetic field. During the experiment, the lithium-ion battery was placed in the Helmholtz coil, and its placement could be illustrated in Fig. 3.

Magnetic field intensity is a physical quantity introduced to facilitate the analysis of the relationship between magnetic field and current. It is a vector, expressed by H, and its relationship with magnetic induction intensity is
(1)
H =
B
μ
where μ is the permeability of the magnetic medium.

According to Ampere Circuital Theorem: in a magnetic field, the line integral of the H vector along any closed curve is equal to the algebraic sum of the currents enclosed in the closed curve.
(2)
∮ H × cos α × d l = ∑ I
where α means the very angle between the tangent direction of the curve and direction of the magnetic field of the point.

Due to the symmetry relationship, the magnetic field strength at each point on the concentric circle is equal, and the direction of the magnetic field strength is along the tangent direction of the concentric circle, i.e
α = 0
(3)
∮ H × cos α × d l = H × 2 π r = N I
where N means the number of the coil turns. Thus, the magnetic field strength value at a certain point is
(4)
H =
N I
2 π r

Set the output current of the experimental power supply according to the experimental needs. According to the current formula corresponding to the magnetic field:
(5)
H = 3 . 95 × I
mT
where H means the magnetic induction intensity, and I means the input current of coil.
The intensity of the magnetic field generated is 3.95 mT, 19.75 mT, and 39.5 mT, when the output current is 1 A, 5 A, and 10 A respectively.

In order to investigate the charge–discharge performance and internal resistance performance under magnetic field effect and without magnetic field effect, this experimental method includes two parts. The first experiment was conducted without magnetic field effect, and the second experiment was conducted with magnetic field effect. The experiments take a 18650 lithium-ion battery as the experimental object, and the experimental equipment is mainly MACCOR battery test equipment and Helmholtz coil equipment. The data acquisition time is set to be 0.1 s. The voltage, current, capacity, energy of the battery is measured during the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge and discharge test method without magnetic field effect
ce:section.ce:para: In the experiment, a 18650 lithium-ion battery was connected with MACCOR battery charging and discharging test system through battery clamp, and the 18650 lithium-ion battery was placed in the thermostat to keep the experimental environment temperature at 25 °C. Before the test, the lithium-ion battery should be checked to be free of damage and crack. The surface of the battery should be flat, free of deformation and stains, and the marks should be correct and clear. After the inspection, the charge and discharge test could be carried out. Specific experimental methods were as follows:
(1) Standard charge and discharge experiments are carried out at 25 °C in a thermostat;
(2) Discharge the battery at the rate of 1/3 C, 1/2 C and 1 C respectively. When the cut-off voltage reaches 2.75 V, the discharge stops;
(3) Rest for 1 h and keep the lithium-ion battery stationary until the voltage and temperature reach a stable state;

(4) When charging, constant current-constant voltage charging is adopted. Firstly, charge at 1/3 C, 1/2 C and 1 C respectively. when the battery voltage reaches 4.2 V, turn to constant voltage charging, when the current is less than 0.05 C, stop charging. Let it stand for 1 h;
(5) Cycle (1) to (4) three times, and take the experimental results of the third time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge and discharge test method with magnetic field effect
ce:section.ce:para: The battery was placed in the Helmholtz coil device, and the environment temperature of the battery was kept at the same level as the first experiment. Different DC currents generate different magnetic field intensity. When the current of the DC power supply is 1 A, 5 A and 10 A, the magnetic induction intensity generated is 3.95 mT, 19.75 mT and 39.50 mT respectively. The following experimental steps are carried out according to (1) ∼ (5) described in paragraph 3.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internal resistance test method without magnetic field effect
ce:section.ce:para:
The HPPC method is adopted in this experiment, that is, pulse experiments are carried out to obtain the discharge resistance of lithium-ion batteries under different SOC conditions. The internal resistance of battery is mainly composed of ohmic internal resistance and polarization internal resistance. The existence of internal resistance leads to the formation of ohmic internal resistance. Ohmic internal resistance is the sum of internal resistance [19]. The polarization resistance is that the chemical substances inside the battery participate in the chemical reaction. When charging and discharging, the battery has polarization reaction, so the polarization resistance is produced [20]. Fig. 4 shows the discharge process in the HPPC method when the SOC is 90%. During the experiment, the battery was loaded with a pulse discharge current, and the pulse time was 10 s. The voltage will immediately drop sharply, that is, from
U
A
to
U
B
. The step change of this part of voltage is caused by the ohmic internal resistance of the battery, which is the ohmic internal resistance of the lithium-ion battery. Under different SOC values, the ohmic internal resistance is different. Then the terminal voltage decreases slowly from
U
B
to
U
C
, and this part of the voltage change is caused by the internal resistance to polarization.
The specific process of its testing is as follows:
(1) Firstly, the battery is starting to be charged by a constant voltage and a constant current charging method, and then the battery is placed in a 25 °C thermostat to stand for 1 h;
(2) Constantly drain the battery with 1 C current for 6 min, the SOC of the battery is 90%, and stand for 1 h;

(3) Discharge with 1 C current pulse for 10 s, record
U
A
,
U
B
,
U
C
, and then stand for 40 s, charge with 1 C current pulse for 10 s, stand for 40 s;
(5) When the discharge voltage becomes to be 2.75 V, the discharge process is then stopped, and the SOC value of the battery is 0%.

According to Ohm’s law, the Ohm internal resistance and polarization internal resistance could be calculated and derived by obeying the following formula:
(6)
R
o
=
U
A
−
U
B
I
(7)
R
j
=
U
B
−
U
C
I
(8)
R =
R
o
+
R
j
where
R
o
means the ohmic resistance under discharge,
R
j
means the polarization resistance under discharge,
U
A
,
U
B
and
U
C
are the terminal voltages corresponding to points A, B and C, and I means the impulse current.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internal resistance test method with magnetic field effect
ce:section.ce:para: The battery was put into the Helmholtz coil device, so that the environment of the battery was consistent with the ambient temperature of the first experiment, and different DC currents were introduced to generate the corresponding magnetic field intensity. The following experimental steps are carried out in accordance with (1) ∼ (5).

Fig. 5(a) shows the discharge capacity under different magnetic induction intensities, with discharge rates of 1 C, 1/2 C and 1/3 C, respectively. The difference in the actual capacity released by the battery under different rates is very obvious, and the discharge capacity of the battery under 1 C is significantly greater than 1/2 C and 1/3 C. Due to the high discharge rate, the flow speed of lithium ions in the battery is accelerated, but the flow channel of lithium ions is fixed, resulting in a large number of lithium ions cannot get sufficient chemical reaction, and the larger the discharge rate, the bigger the discharge current, the smaller the discharge time required, so the discharge capacity of the battery decreases with the increase of discharge rate. When the magnetic induction intensity is 0 mT, the discharge capacity is 2.46 Ah at 1/3 C, 2.26 Ah at 1/2 C and 2.10 Ah at 1 C. It can be found that under the same magnetic induction intensities, the discharge capacity decreases with discharge rate. At the same discharge rate, taking the 1/3C rate as an example, the discharge capacity is found to be 2.46 Ah when the battery is not placed in the magnetic field, that is, the magnetic induction intensity is 0mT. While the magnetic induction intensity increases to 3.98 mT, the discharge capacity is 2.51 Ah, which increases by 2.03%. When the magnetic induction intensity increases to 19.75 mT, the discharge capacity is 2.66 Ah, which increases by 8.13%. When the magnetic induction intensity increases to 39.50 mT, the discharge capacity is 2.89 Ah, which increases by 17.48%. It can be seen that the discharge capacity of the battery gradually increases with magnetic induction intensity.

During charging process, the charging capacity of the battery also increases with the increase of magnetic induction intensity, as shown in Fig. 5(b). This phenomenon indicates that the external magnetic field could promote charge–discharge capacity.

Fig. 6(a) illustrates the discharge energy of the battery imposing different values of magnetic induction intensity. The discharge energy gradually decreases with the battery discharge rate. At the same discharge rate, taking 1/3 C rate as an example, when the battery is not placed in the magnetic field, the discharge energy is 8.66 Wh. When the magnetic induction intensity increases to 3.98 mT, the discharge energy is 8.85 Wh, which increases by 2.20%. When the magnetic induction intensity increases to 19.75 mT, the discharge energy is 9.40 Wh, which increases by 8.55%. When the magnetic induction intensity increases to 39.50 mT, the discharge energy is 10.28 Wh, which increases by 18.71%. Similar to the discharge capacity, the discharge energy increases with magnetic induction intensity.

Fig. 6(b) showed the charging energy of the battery under different magnetic induction intensities. The charging energy of the battery increases with magnetic induction intensity, and the overall trend is basically the same as that in Fig. 5(b). But the charge energy is higher than the discharge energy. The magnetic field has the radiation characteristics of wave particles. When the external magnetic field acts on the battery, the interior of the battery is magnetized and many small magnetic dipoles generated, which make the particle materials in the battery magnetically arrange and improve the ion conductivity, so as to accelerate the flow and diffusion of ions near the electrode, and then change the working performance of lithium-ion battery. Meanwhile, it also improves the energy density.

Fig. 7 shows the change of voltage during discharge, mainly including FDRV and SDRV. The horizontal axis is SOC and the vertical axis is terminal voltage. The whole figure can be divided into three areas: steep descent area, slow descent area and steep descent area. The voltage in the first region decreases rapidly with the increase of time. In the second region of the figure, the voltage decreases slowly with the time. In the third region, the voltage drops rapidly to the cut-off voltage. During the operation of lithium-ion battery, most working hours are in the second region, and only a small part of working hours are in the first and third regions. The duration of working time in the second region is an important reflection of the health state of lithium-ion battery, which indicates that the addition of magnetic field effect does not change the overall trend of voltage curve. When the SOC is 90% ∼ 100%, the voltage drops rapidly; When the SOC of the battery is 20% ∼ 90%, the discharge curve tends to be flat and enters the discharge platform. This is because in the discharge process, the negative electrode has oxidation reaction, releases electrons, and the positive electrode has reduction reaction, so the voltage distinction between the electrodes gradually decreases. When the discharge is almost over, the voltage suddenly drops to the discharge cut-off voltage.

The internal resistance represents the degree of resistance to the current flowing through the battery. Internal resistance is a key parameter of lithium-ion batteries, which directly affects the working voltage, working current, output energy and power of the battery. The less internal resistance the better. The internal resistance is not a constant value, but varies with the composition of the active substance, electrolyte concentration, battery temperature, and discharge time. While the current changes, the ohmic resistance causes an instantaneous change, while the polarization resistance slowly affects the battery voltage. Fig. 8(a) and (b) reflect the changes of the internal ohmic resistance and polarization resistance with SOC during discharge. It can be clearly seen that the internal ohmic resistance is greater than the internal polarization resistance, and the internal ohmic resistance of the battery gradually decreases with the increase of the SOC. As can be seen and spotted from Fig. 8(a), when the SOC changes from 90% to 40%, the ohmic resistance changes little. When the SOC is below 40%, the ohmic resistance rises sharply. To sum up, when a lithium-ion battery is charged in a certain environment, its ohmic internal resistance remains stable in a certain SOC range, until the SOC continues to decrease to a certain value during the discharge process, and then the ohmic internal resistance rise sharply as the battery continues to discharge.

In Fig. 8(b), when the SOC changes from 90% to 60%, the change of polarization resistance is not obvious. When SOC = 50%, the internal polarization resistance decreases suddenly. In the range of 50% to 20%, the change of polarization resistance is not obvious. When SOC changes from 20% to 10%, the polarization resistance increases sharply, and the phenomenon of “upwarping” appears. It is also found that the greater the magnetic induction, the smaller the polarization resistance.

Because the internal resistance measured in this test is DC internal resistance, its size is related to the cathode and anode materials, electrolyte conductivity and SOC of the battery, and the electrolyte in the battery is mainly conductive through the ions in the electrolyte. In the process of discharge, due to the action of magnetic field, the battery magnetized and many small magnetic dipoles generated, which make the granular materials in the battery magnetically arranged, improve the ion conductivity and make the ion move faster. Therefore, the ohmic internal resistance in the electrolyte becomes smaller. The internal resistance is the main component of ohmic internal resistance. As the ion moves faster, the chemical reaction speed of the battery also becomes faster, so the polarization internal resistance becomes smaller. To sum up, the ohmic internal resistance and polarization internal resistance decrease with the increase of magnetic induction.
Based on the charge and discharge performance and internal resistance with and without magnetic field effect, the conclusions we could get are as following:
(1) The trends of terminal voltage during charge and discharge process with and without magnetic field are basically the same.

(2) When the magnetic field effect acts on the battery, the interior of the battery magnetized and many small magnetic dipoles are generated. Then the ions in the battery magnetically are arranged, which improves the conductivity of ions, accelerates the flow and diffusion of ions near the electrode, and changes the working performance. The charge–discharge performance with imposing magnetic field is better than that without imposing magnetic field. The discharge capacity, charge capacity, discharge energy and charge energy increase obviously, and they all increase with magnetic induction intensity.

(3) During the discharge process, as the inside of the battery is magnetized, the conductivity of ions in electrolyte is improved, and the movement speed of ions becomes faster. Hence, the ohmic internal resistance in the electrolyte becomes smaller. As the ion moves faster, the chemical reaction speed also becomes faster, the polarization internal resistance is also smaller. Therefore, the ohmic internal resistance and polarization internal resistance will decrease with the increase of magnetic induction.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This paper thanks to the support from Shanghai 2021 Science and Technology Innovation Action Plan Project (No. 21DZ2304600).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mini review on cellulose-based composite separators for lithium-ion batteries: Recent progress and perspectives

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Experimental investigation on the charge–discharge performance of the commercial lithium-ion batteries

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of Lorentz force on the electrochemical performance of lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: CHENG H M, WANG F M, CHU J P. Effect of Lorentz force on the electrochemical performance of lithium-ion batteries. Electrochemistry Communications, 2017,76: 63-66.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Li-ion battery ageing model parameter: SEI layer analysis using magnetic field probing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: SINGH P, KHARE N, CHATURVEDI P K. Li-ion battery ageing model parameter: SEI layer analysis using magnetic field probing. Engineering Science and Technology, an International Journal, 2018,21(1): 35-42.
DUNCA S, CREANGA D E, AILIESEI O, et al. Microorganisms growth with magnetic fluids. Journal of Magnetism & Magnetic Materials, 2005,289: 445-447.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Disappearance of growth advantage in stationary phase (GASP) phenomenon under a high magnetic field

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: OKUNO K, FUJINAMI R, ANO T, et al. Disappearance of growth advantage in stationary phase (GASP) phenomenon under a high magnetic field. Bioelectrochemistry, 2001,53(2): 165-169.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic field-suppressed lithium dendrite growth for stable lithium-metal batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shen K, Wang Z, Bi X, et al. Magnetic Field-Suppressed Lithium Dendrite Growth for Stable Lithium-Metal Batteries. Advanced energy materials, 2019, 9(20):1900260.1-1900260.8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetically aligned graphite electrodes for high-rate performance li-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: BILLAUD J, BOUVILLE F, MAGRINI T, et al. Magnetically aligned graphite electrodes for high-rate performance Li-ion batteries. Nature Energy, 2016,1(8): 1-6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigation of PEMFC under static magnetic field: Temperature, relative humidity and performance
